comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Mathematical Review — Complexity of Collection in Polycyclic Groups

**No `solution.tex` exists.** This is a "determine the quantity" problem: find the worst-case
complexity $T(\ell,n,b)$ of the collection algorithm (input length $\ell$, Hirsch length $n$,
exponent bit-length $b$). A complete answer needs **both** an upper bound and a matching/lower
bound, since the word "worst-case" demands tightness.

### Decomposition into sub-lemmas (issues created)
- **rm-00806-2** Termination + quantitative collection-step count via a well-founded potential
  $\Phi=\sum_i c_i(\text{weight of uncollected }g_i)$; must handle power relations
  $g_i^{r_i}=w_{i,i}$ re-introducing/lengthening generators.
- **rm-00806-3 (crux)** Bound the bit-length $B$ of the largest *intermediate* exponent.
  Iterated conjugation multiplies exponents. Verified example:
  $G=\langle g_1,g_2\mid g_2^{g_1}=g_2^2\rangle$, input $g_1^{-\ell}g_2 g_1^{\ell}=g_2^{2^\ell}$
  gives exponent bit-length $\Theta(\ell)$ with $n=2$, $b=O(1)$. So a "poly($\ell,n,b$) with
  constant-size integers" claim is **false**; the answer must carry an exponent-blow-up term.
- **rm-00806-4** Fix the cost model (bit operations, $M(B)$); adopt Gebhardt-style binary
  collection of powers (repeated squaring) to avoid an extra factor exponential in $b$. State
  it in an `algorithm` box with correctness/termination/complexity proofs (ALGORITHM RULE).
- **rm-00806-5** Matching lower bound: exhibit a worst-case family realizing $\Omega(T)$;
  generalize the $\mathbb Z\rtimes\mathbb Z$ example in $n$ and $b$.
- **rm-00806-6** Two regimes: finite ($\max r_i=R<\infty$, exponents bounded by $\log R$,
  poly complexity) vs. infinite (exponent blow-up dominates). Both must be stated and proved,
  with the mixed-presentation boundary handled.

### Key references to cite
- C. C. Sims, *Computation with Finitely Presented Groups*, CUP 1994, Ch. 9 (consistency,
  collection, termination).
- Leedham-Green & Soicher, "Collection from the left and other strategies", J. Symb. Comput. 9
  (1990).
- V. Gebhardt, "Efficient collection in infinite polycyclic groups", J. Symb. Comput. 34 (2002)
  (binary/symbolic collection; infinite case).

### Difficulty
The dominant difficulty is **rm-00806-3** (exponent growth governed by products of integer
conjugation matrices on the abelian sections); **rm-00806-5** (proving the blow-up is
unavoidable, not an analysis artifact) is the other hard part. The remaining issues are
moderate but materially change the stated answer.

Full analysis attached as the "Critic Analysis" document.
